retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of injury may be decreased by obtaining an intraoperative cholangiogram, especially in cases of gangrenous cholecystitis or empyema of the gallbladder. [7] [8] The Critical View of Safety, a technique helping surgeons to identify the cystic duct and artery, is utilized to minimize bile duct injuries and associated morbidity from bile leaks and strictures. [9] Understanding the anatomy of the blood supply to the bile duct is paramount to its repair. [10] Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma are the most common causes of malignant strictures. Benign causes include:

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration cytology is highly sensitive and specific in diagnosing malignant biliary strictures, particularly when associated with a mass. Still, it is ineffective in ruling out malignancy. [5] Endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration or tissue sampling at endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) doesn't carry the risk of spreading malignant cells, which is a concern with a percutaneous needle biopsy. Ductal dilatation is a major feature of imaging for biliary strictures. [1] Imaging of suspected biliary strictures can begin with a transabdominal ultrasound, which detects biliary dilatation and is highly sensitive for detecting biliary obstruction and the level of obstruction. However, it has a low yield for the detection of strictures. Malignant biliary strictures typically cause more severe ductal dilation than benign strictures. [5] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant causes (more common than benign biliary strictures): Pancreatic head cancer Cholangiocarcinoma Ampullary carcinoma Gallbladder carcinoma Hepatocellular carcinoma Lymphoma Metastasis to regional solid organs and lymph nodes [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary strictures refer to narrowed intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal system segments. When narrowed, they impede the normal antegrade flow of bile, causing proximal dilatation, resulting in clinical and pathological sequelae of biliary obstruction. Patients with chronic biliary strictures present a unique challenge when malignancy is suspected. Diagnosing and managing patients with biliary strictures potentially encompasses an interprofessional approach, including interventional endoscopists, surgeons, and interventional radiologists. [1] See Image. Illustration of Bile Duct Stricture.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Biliary Ducts -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bile, a digestive fluid produced and secreted by the liver, is transported by a series of branching bile ducts known collectively as the biliary tree. At the cellular level, several narrow tubular channels called canaliculi collect the bile generated by each hepatocyte. These canaliculi drain into an intralobular bile duct which collects all the bile from each lobule, the functional unit of the liver. Intralobular ducts then drain into the interlobular ducts, which are located between lobules. The interlobular ducts merge to form the two main bile ducts of the liver: the right hepatic duct (RHD) and the left hepatic duct (LHD). Extrahepatically, the RHD and LHD coalesce to form the common hepatic duct (CHD), which travels within the hepatoduodenal ligament until coming into contact with the cystic duct, the bile duct, which connects to the gallbladder. The CHD and cystic duct merge to form the common bile duct (CBD). The hepatopancreatic ampulla, also called the hepatopancreatic duct or ampulla of Vater, is a spherical structure located at the site of the confluence of the common bile duct and pancreatic duct, marking the entry point of bile into the second portion of the duodenum. This is controlled by the smooth muscle fibers of the sphincter of Oddi, which opens at the duodenal papilla, allowing bile to flow into the small intestine. Alternatively, bile can travel into the gallbladder for storage via the cystic duct. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Bile duct strictures can be congenital or acquired. [2] [3] Acquired strictures are further classified as either benign or malignant. [4] A wide range of benign acquired conditions cause bile duct strictures, contributing to 30% of biliary strictures, including iatrogenic strictures, which comprise the majority of benign biliary strictures. [5] Misidentification of the bile duct as the cystic duct during laparoscopic cholecystectomy leads to bile duct injury, which may be partial or complete. The long-term sequelae of these injuries lead to the formation of benign biliary strictures. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary strictures are characterized by narrowing of a bile duct segment associated with proximal ductal dilatation, except in primary or secondary sclerosing cholangitis, where sclerosis prevents the ducts from dilating. Obstruction of bile flow leads to elevation of serum bilirubin levels with clinical and laboratory features of obstructive jaundice. Stasis of bile is a significant risk factor for ascending cholangitis. [17] Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography or endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography shows that benign strictures have tapered margins with smooth and symmetric borders. On the other hand, malignant strictures have shouldering of the margins with irregular and asymmetric borders. [12] Malignant strictures typically involve a longer duct segment than benign strictures. Malignant strictures appear to enhance contrast-enhanced cross-sectional imaging. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Choledochal Cyst -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The exact etiology of a choledochal cyst remains unknown, which has led to the postulation of multiple theories. The most popular theory is Babbitt's, which states that the cyst develops from an anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction (APBJ). APBJ is when the biliary and the pancreatic duct join 1 to 2 cm proximal to the sphincter of Oddi. As a result, the long channel formed is not covered by the sphincter and allows for the backflow and mixing of pancreatic and biliary secretions, which leads to the activation of pancreatic enzymes. This results in a rise in pressure, which eventually causes dilation, inflammation, epithelial damage, dysplasia, and malignancy of the biliary tree. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The Strasberg-Bismuth classification defines injuries and biliary strictures based on their anatomic location within the biliary system relating to the biliary confluence. [15] Type A, the most common, is a leak from the cystic duct or small ducts in the gallbladder or liver bed. Type B is an occlusion of an aberrant right hepatic duct, while type C is transection of this aberrant right hepatic duct with subsequent leakage. These injuries often occur in conjunction with a cystic duct that drains into an aberrant right hepatic duct. At the level of the aberrant right hepatic duct draining into the common hepatic or common bile duct, it may be misidentified as the cystic duct and transected. Type D is an injury to the lateral bile duct involving less than 50% of the duct circumference. Type E injuries are defined as strictures to the hepatic ducts and are further classified by the proximal extent. Type E1 strictures have a common duct stump of greater than 2 cm. Type E2 strictures have less than 2 cm of common duct available for anastomosis. Type E3 strictures occur at the confluence. Type E4 strictures indicate a separation of the right and left hepatic ducts due to the destruction of the confluence. Type E5 stricture is due to the injury of an aberrant right hepatic duct with concomitant stricture of the common hepatic duct. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: ERCP can determine the etiology, provide tissue samples for cytology and histology, and facilitate therapeutic interventions, including dilatation and stenting of obstructing biliary (and pancreatic) strictures. However, because of the relatively high risk of post-ERCP pancreatitis, MRCP has replaced ERCP when only a diagnostic study is needed. ERCP is limited to therapeutic interventions. Emerging technologies useful in assessing the etiology of biliary strictures include confocal laser endomicroscopy [23] , fluorescent in-situ hybridization (FISH), [24] direct peroral cholangioscopy, [25] and intraductal ultrasound. [4] [15] [26] 2

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Cystic Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Caroli Disease: is a rare benign autosomal recessive condition that manifests with multifocal cystic dilatation of segmental intrahepatic bile ducts. Patients present with frequent or recurrent bouts of cholangitis or those with portal hypertension and biliary cirrhosis. Ultrasound shows saccular dilatation of intrahepatic bile ducts, intraductal bridging, echogenic septa traversing the dilated bile duct lumen, and small portal venous branches partially or fully surrounded by dilated bile ducts. If the disease is localized, segmentectomy, or lobectomy may be done. In diffuse disease management, usually, with conservative measures, liver transplantation may be an option. [31] An Intrahepatic Pseudocyst: is a rare condition that may occur in the setting of alcoholic pancreatitis. It may require prompt percutaneous or endoscopic drainage or surgical resection if it is large or symptomatic to prevent complications. [32]

retrieved_docs.content: Iatrogenic Cholecystectomy [11] Pancreaticoduodenectomy: Incidence of 4%, especially when there are small-caliber thin-walled ducts [1] Liver transplantation [12] Due to radiation therapy Secondary sclerosing cholangitis due to 5-fluorouracil or bevacizumab chemotherapy (these usually affect the intrahepatic ducts) [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Globally, the incidence of biliary strictures is increasing primarily because of iatrogenic bile duct injuries resulting from the widespread practice of laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The estimated rate of biliary injuries is about 0.7% after laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Most of these injuries are minor injuries or bile leaks. Biliary strictures are rare in the pediatric age group. [16] There is no published difference in the incidence or prevalence of biliary strictures in males compared to females. However, some risk factors, like chronic pancreatitis, are more common in males who more often drink alcohol and smoke.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 743
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to investigate, access, and treat pathology in diverse anatomical regions, and they are organized under the parent clinical actions “Clinical actions A – G.” Within this category, a partial or complete adrenalectomy—or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy—can be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also include excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy procedures that incorporate exploration of the common bile duct are represented in several variants: standard duct exploration, duct exploration with cholangiography, and duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography; these can be executed laparoscopically. retrieved_docs.content: abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), and biliary disorders (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ). Clinical findings such as an abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) or acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) are evaluated with the same imaging and procedural approaches. Understanding the interconnected anatomy of the abdomen and liver, the spectrum of hepatic diseases, their risk factors, and the potential toxicities of substances used in abdominal interventions is essential for safe procedural planning, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1219
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures are defined as a broad group of surgical actions (Clinical actions A–G) used to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions, and each named technique functions either as a primary “method” or as a “possibly‑included method” when combined with adjunctive steps. In the adrenal gland, partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and when coupled with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor, are classified as exploration methods. Biliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration performed with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional sphincterotomy; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is recorded similarly.

This integrated framework aids clinicians in selecting the appropriate exploratory approach based on the anatomical site, underlying condition, and required therapeutic objectives, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2610
retrieved_docs.content: Intraoperative cholangiogram is a specific type of imaging that belongs to the broader category of صورة الأوعية الصفراوية (cholangiographic imaging of the biliary ducts). It is performed during surgery to visualize the biliary tree and assess the anatomy and patency of the bile ducts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1708
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a wide range of surgical techniques that are employed to investigate, decompress, or treat pathology in various anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland exploration such as “Adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of adrenal gland with or without biopsy, transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal (separate procedure),” and a related variant “Adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of adrenal gland with or without biopsy, transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal (separate procedure); with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor,” both of which are identified as specific methods of exploration. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2683
retrieved_docs.content: The US scan of gallbladder is a diagnostic procedure that is performed directly on the organ identified as žlučník. Biliary tract disease denotes pathological conditions that are located in the same organ, žlučník. Recognizing that the gallbladder (žlučník) serves both as the direct procedural site for the ultrasound scan and as the anatomical location of biliary tract disease underscores the relevance of targeted imaging in the assessment and management of these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2252
retrieved_docs.content: Cholecystectomy and common duct exploration is classified as a specific subprocedure within the broader surgical category of استئصال المرارة (gallbladder removal). In the context of استئصال المرارة, صورة الأوعية الصفراوية (imaging of the biliary ducts) is closely related, as this imaging modality is routinely used to assess the anatomy of the biliary tree, guide intra‑operative decision‑making, and ensure complete clearance of stones when a common duct exploration is performed. The integration of صورة الأوعية الصفراوية with cholecystectomy and common duct exploration highlights the importance of combining diagnostic imaging and therapeutic surgery to optimize patient outcomes and reduce postoperative complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 878
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are performed to visualize, assess, and treat pathology within a variety of anatomic regions. Within this category, specific methods are defined, each tailored to the organ system or clinical scenario involved. Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct is a recognized method, and it can be further specified as Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with cholangiography or as Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with choledochoenterostomy. A related biliary approach, Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or removal of calculus—performed with or without cholecystotomy and often accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty—may be included as part of an exploration procedure. Laparoscopic surgery also provides a method for biliary exploration, exemplified by Laparoscopy, surgical; cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct. Cranial

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome has a mapped association with the development of cancer of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection is important for clinicians, as patients diagnosed with Bard‑Pic syndrome may require heightened surveillance for pancreatic malignancy and tailored management strategies to address the potential increased risk.
